POST_PROMPT:
Create a natural, authentic recipe post in English that feels written by a real food blogger, not AI. CRITICAL: Vary your writing style, length, and structure for EACH recipe to appear authentically human-written. JSON STRUCTURE: { "title": "Generate ONE highly clickable, SEO-optimized recipe title (55-90 characters). The title must sound completely natural, like it was written by a professional food blogger, and should be optimized for both Google SEO and Pinterest SEO.\n\nPRIMARY GOALS:\n- Naturally include the primary long-tail keyword.\n- Place the primary keyword near the beginning whenever it sounds natural.\n- Maximize click-through rate (CTR).\n- Match real user search intent.\n- Sound human-written, never AI-generated.\n\nROTATE NATURALLY BETWEEN THESE TITLE STYLES:\n\n- Classic:\n Grandma's Homemade Chocolate Chip Cookies\n\n- Best:\n The Best Moist Banana Bread You'll Ever Make\n\n- Easy:\n Easy 30-Minute Chicken Stir Fry for Busy Weeknights\n\n- Quick:\n 15-Minute Honey Garlic Salmon Recipe\n\n- Homemade:\n Homemade Garlic Bread with Crispy Edges\n\n- Healthy:\n Healthy Greek Chicken Bowls for Meal Prep\n\n- Authentic:\n Authentic Mexican Street Corn (Elote) Recipe\n\n- Comfort Food:\n Cozy Homemade Beef Stew for Cold Nights\n\n- Creamy:\n Creamy Garlic Parmesan Pasta Everyone Loves\n\n- Crispy:\n Crispy Oven Baked Chicken Wings Recipe\n\n- Copycat:\n Better Than Restaurant Garlic Butter Shrimp\n\n- One-Pot:\n One-Pot Creamy Tomato Pasta Recipe\n\n- Air Fryer:\n Crispy Air Fryer Chicken Tenders Recipe\n\n- No-Bake:\n Easy No-Bake Cheesecake Recipe\n\n- Seasonal:\n Perfect Fall Pumpkin Bread Recipe\n\n- Beginner-Friendly:\n Foolproof Homemade Pizza Dough for Beginners\n\n- Why You'll Love It:\n Why Everyone Loves This Creamy Chicken Pasta\n\nSEO & PINTEREST OPTIMIZATION:\n- Prioritize long-tail keywords that people actually search.\n- Optimize for Pinterest search behavior.\n- Use emotional power words only when they fit naturally.\n- Create curiosity without using misleading clickbait.\n- Promise a clear benefit when appropriate.\n- Vary title length and wording across generations.\n- Avoid repetitive title patterns.\n- Avoid keyword stuffing.\n- Avoid ALL CAPS.\n- Avoid excessive punctuation.\n- Never generate generic titles.\n\nCOMMON SEARCH TERMS TO NATURALLY INCORPORATE WHEN RELEVANT:\n- easy\n- homemade\n- healthy\n- best\n- creamy\n- crispy\n- authentic\n- quick\n- one-pot\n- air fryer\n- slow cooker\n- no bake\n- meal prep\n- copycat\n- family dinner\n- weeknight dinner\n- comfort food\n- beginner\n\nPOWER WORDS (USE NATURALLY, DO NOT FORCE):\nEasy, Best, Ultimate, Homemade, Delicious, Flavorful, Juicy, Crispy, Creamy, Perfect, Irresistible, Foolproof, Authentic, Cozy, Healthy, Simple.\n\nOUTPUT REQUIREMENTS:\n- Return ONLY the final title.\n- No quotation marks.\n- No numbering.\n- No emojis.\n- No explanation." "promptIMG": "Food photography: [dish name], appetizing presentation, natural lighting, kitchen setting, documentary style, realistic colors --ar 4:3 --quality 2", "description": "Write 150-250 character natural description (VARY the length): Natural Formula: - What it is + why it's good - One special ingredient or technique - When to serve it Example: This creamy chicken pasta is comfort food at its best. Ready in 30 minutes with a rich garlic sauce. Perfect for busy weeknights! ✓ Write like texting a friend ✓ No formulaic structure ✓ Be genuine and specific", "hashtags": "3-5 relevant hashtags (VARY the number): #recipename #category #benefit Keep it simple and natural", "board_name": "Prioritize boards matching these high-affinity categories: Pizza, Sandwich, Soup, Salad, Seafood. Only use existing boards for other categories.", -"Pinterest board name optimized for SEO (2-5 words, Title Case): - Based on recipe category and main ingredient - Match what Pinterest users search for - Examples: \"Easy Chicken Recipes\", \"Healthy Soup Ideas\", \"Comfort Food Dinners\", \"Quick Weeknight Meals\" - NO hashtags, NO punctuation, just the board name", BOARD PRIORITY (by audience affinity): - Pizza recipes → affinity 16.3x (HIGH PRIORITY) - Sandwich ideas → affinity 14.5x (HIGH PRIORITY) - Soup recipes → affinity 10.9x - Salad recipes → affinity 7.8x - Seafood dinners → affinity 8.1x - Vegetable sides → existing strong board "pin_variations": [ { "title": "...", "description": "..." }, { "title": "...", "description": "..." }, { "title": "...", "description": "..." }, { "title": "...", "description": "..." } ] Instructions: Create 4 pin variations. TITLES — CRITICAL RULES: - ABSOLUTELY NO EMOJIS in titles (they render as broken boxes on pin images). - Plain text only: letters, numbers, basic punctuation (- , ' ? ! &). - Max 70 characters. - The primary keyword must appear naturally in every title, as close to the beginning as it can sound natural. - Pick 4 DIFFERENT angles from this list (rotate freely between recipes, never use the same 4 angles twice in a row): 1. Curiosity question: "Why Does Restaurant Garlic Chicken Taste Better Than Yours?" 2. Secret/tip: "The One Trick That Makes Creamy Garlic Chicken Foolproof" 3. Time/ease benefit: "15-Minute Creamy Garlic Chicken for Busy Weeknights" 4. Comparison: "Skillet vs Oven Garlic Chicken - Which Wins on Flavor?" 5. Identity/pain point: "Still Making Dry Chicken? This Creamy Skillet Fixes It" 6. Social proof: "The Creamy Garlic Chicken My Family Begs For Every Week" 7. Direct benefit: "Creamy Garlic Chicken So Good You'll Skip Takeout" 8. Seasonal/occasion: "Cozy Fall Dinner: Creamy Garlic Chicken and Spinach" DESCRIPTIONS: - 2-3 sentences, natural and engaging - answer the title partially to create a curiosity gap. - Emojis ALLOWED here (2-3 max, natural placement) - descriptions are text-only on Pinterest, they render fine. - End with a clear call-to-action ("Save this for dinner inspo", "Tap to get the full recipe"). - Add 4-6 relevant hashtags at the very end. - Focus on keywords people actually search on Pinterest. - No repetition of sentences between the 4 variations. "difficulty": "Easy|Medium|Hard", "prep_time": 15, "cook_time": 30, "servings": 4, "ingredients": [ "Clear measurements + ingredient + state", "In order of use when logical" ], "yield": "e.g. Approximately 6 cups, 4 servings", "introduction": "Write 2-4 paragraphs (150-250 words total) personal introduction about this recipe: - Open with your personal connection, backstory, or when you first made/discovered this dish - Explain what makes this version better or different from standard recipes - Mention 1-2 specific techniques or secrets that make it succeed - Set expectations: what the finished result tastes/feels/looks like ✓ First person, conversational tone ('I've been making this...', 'the trick I learned...') ✓ Include at least one honest detail (a past failure, a discovery, a family memory) ✓ Sound like a home cook sharing with a friend, not a recipe developer pitching", "instructions": [ { "step": 1, "instruction": "80-130 words: detailed action + technique explanation + WHY this step matters + what to watch for + common mistake to avoid at this step. Go beyond just listing what to do — explain what is happening and why it produces the right result.", "timing": "X minutes", "doneness_cue": "Specific visual, textural, or aromatic cue that this step is correctly done" }, { "step": 2, "instruction": "80-130 words: specific quantities + technique detail + temperature or timing explanation + what changes if you rush it or skip it.", "timing": "X minutes", "doneness_cue": "How to know this step is complete — be precise and sensory (color, sound, texture, smell)" } ], "why_this_recipe_works": { "section": "Natural section title like 'Understanding Why This Formula Succeeds' or 'the Science Behind This Recipe'", "content": "

3-4 detailed paragraphs (400-600 words total) explaining the science or technique that makes this recipe succeed. Cover ingredient interactions, cooking chemistry, temperature effects, Maillard reactions, emulsification, or whatever applies. Educational but accessible — explain the WHY behind each key step in depth.

behind it — make the reader understand WHY, not just WHAT" }, { "tip": "A third tip covering equipment, timing, or temperature", "reason": "70-100 words: detailed explanation with real consequences of not following it" } ], "common_mistakes_to_avoid": [ { "mistake": "Name the common mistake clearly", "why_it_happens": "50-70 words: explain the psychology or assumption behind why cooks make this error", "fix": "70-100 words: specific, actionable fix with exact technique, temperature, or timing correction" } ], "variations": [ { "name": "Variation name", "description": "80-110 words: how to adapt the recipe — ingredient swaps, technique changes, flavor profile shift, and what to expect differently in texture and taste", "technical_note": "40-60 words: technique adjustment, timing change, or texture difference to watch for" } ], "nutrition": { "calories_per_serving": "420 kcal", "protein_grams": "28g", "carbohydrates_grams": "35g", "fat_grams": "18g", "fiber_grams": "4g", "sodium_mg": "580mg", "note": "Nutritional values are estimates and may vary based on specific ingredients used." }, "storage_and_reheating": { "room_temperature": "How long it safely keeps at room temperature and how to cover/store it", "refrigerator": "Store in airtight container for up to X days. Note how flavor or texture changes when served cold and whether to reheat or serve cold.", "freezer": "Freeze for up to X months. How to wrap properly, prevent freezer burn, thaw safely, and restore texture after freezing.", "reheating": "Specific reheating method (stovetop/microwave/oven), ideal temperature, time, what to add if needed (splash of liquid, etc.), and what to avoid", "make_ahead_strategy": "What steps can be done 1-2 days ahead, how to store the partially prepared dish, and what to do the day of serving" }, "faq": [ { "question": "A common question readers have about this specific recipe (troubleshooting, substitutions, timing, storage)", "answer": "80-120 words: specific, practical answer that genuinely solves the problem. Include the WHY, not just the WHAT. 8-10 FAQ entries required." }, { "question": "Another practical question about technique, ingredients, or results", "answer": "80-120 words: helpful, detailed answer based on real cooking knowledge" } ], "conclusion": "1-2 sentence warm closing. Invite readers to try the recipe and share results.", "seo": { "primary_keyword": "main 2-5 word search phrase for this specific recipe (e.g. 'southern pecan pie recipe')", "secondary_keywords": [ "related keyword phrase 1", "related keyword phrase 2", "how to make [recipe name]", "best [recipe name] recipe", "easy [recipe name]" ], "internal_links": [ {"text": "related recipe or technique text", "anchor": "anchor_slug_here"} ], "focus_keyword_density": "1.0-1.5%", "reading_level": "8th grade", "word_count_total": "approximately X words" } } TARGET AUDIENCE PROFILE: - American women, 55+ years old - Home cooks, not professional chefs - Cook for family, value simplicity - Prefer: classic comfort food, familiar ingredients - Avoid: trendy/exotic techniques, unusual ingredients - Tone: warm, practical, like advice from a neighbor CRITICAL AUTHENTICITY RULES: ✓ Use NATURAL language - write like a food blogger, not SEO copywriter ✓ Keyword density: 4-8 times MAXIMUM (not 10-15!) ✓ Imperfect is better - sound human, not AI ✓ Personal touches - "I love", "My family", "Works great when" ✓ Vary paragraph length - some 1 sentence, some 4 sentences ✓ Use contractions: "it's", "you'll", "don't" ✓ Occasional opinions: "I think", "in my experience" MANDATORY MINIMUM CONTENT PER FIELD (NON-NEGOTIABLE): ✓ "introduction": REQUIRED string, 250-350 words — NEVER null, NEVER omit this field ✓ "why_this_recipe_works": REQUIRED OBJECT (never a plain string!) with keys: - "section": section title string - "content": 400-600 words HTML string (3-4 paragraphs cooking science) - "ingredient_notes": object with 5-8 key ingredient names, each 70-100 words ✓ instructions: MINIMUM 9 steps, each step 100-150 words (include technique rationale, not just actions) ✓ pro_tips: 7-10 tips, each reason field 70-100 words of explanation ✓ common_mistakes_to_avoid: 5-7 mistakes, each fix 70-100 words ✓ variations: 5-7 variations, each description 80-110 words with technical_note ✓ storage_and_reheating: each field (room_temperature, refrigerator, freezer, reheating, make_ahead_strategy) 80-120 words ✓ faq: 8-10 Q&A pairs, each answer 80-120 words (troubleshooting, substitutions, timing) ✓ TOTAL: 4000-6000 words of actual content (ALL fields combined) NATURAL WRITING STYLE: ✓ First person occasionally: "I like to...", "I usually..." ✓ Second person casually: "You'll love...", "Try adding..." ✓ Mix sentence lengths ✓ Some shorter paragraphs (2 sentences) ✓ Some longer paragraphs (5 sentences) ✓ Transition words naturally: "Also,", "Plus,", "Another thing," ✓ Questions occasionally: "Want to make it spicier?" JSON VALIDATION (CRITICAL): ✓ Valid JSON only ✓ Double quotes for strings ✓ Escape quotes: \" ✓ Numbers without quotes: "prep_time": 15 ✓ No trailing commas ✓ Boolean: true/false (no quotes) ALLOWED HTML: ✓

    • ✗ No heading tags, div, span, or inline CSS INTELLIGENCE: ✓ Fill missing details logically ✓ Authentic techniques for cuisine type ✓ Realistic timing ✓ Appropriate suggestions ✓ Cultural accuracy Source Text: {SOURCE_TEXT}

NEVER leave a question or answer field blank, empty, or with placeholder whitespace. ✓ TOTAL: 4000-6000 words of actual content (ALL fields combined) NATURAL WRITING STYLE: ✓ First person occasionally: "I like to...", "I usually..." ✓ Second person casually: "You'll love...", "Try adding..." ✓ Mix sentence lengths ✓ Some shorter paragraphs (2 sentences) ✓ Some longer paragraphs (5 sentences) ✓ Transition words naturally: "Also,", "Plus,", "Another thing," ✓ Questions occasionally: "Want to make it spicier?" IMG_PROMPT_1:
A photograph of {title}, shot by a home food blogger for her personal recipe blog — it must look like a REAL photo taken in a REAL home kitchen, never like a commercial ad or AI render.

CAMERA & LOOK: shot on a DSLR with a 50mm f/2.0 lens. Focus on the front edge of the dish; the back of the dish already softens naturally. Natural handheld framing, very slightly imperfect composition. Subtle fine grain. True-to-life but RICH colors: the food's natural colors are deep, saturated where the ingredients really are colorful, and appetizing — never washed-out, pale, hazy, or flat. Balanced exposure with real contrast (deep shadows AND bright highlights). NOT artificially oversaturated, no HDR glow, no studio gloss.

LIGHT: one window with soft but DIRECTIONAL daylight from the side, nothing else — the kind of light that sculpts the food with gentle contrast, cozy moody food-blog style. Natural shadows with visible direction and depth. Some areas of the frame fall darker — that's normal in a real kitchen and gives the photo dimension. No rim light, no glow, no golden amber wash, no flat white haze.

THE FOOD: one serving of the dish, plated simply on an everyday plate a real family owns (plain ceramic, slightly worn stoneware — vary the dish style naturally to suit the recipe). The food looks homemade and delicious but IMPERFECT: uneven edges, frosting or sauce spread by hand not piped perfectly, an irregular crumb, a few crumbs and a small smudge near the plate. It should trigger "I could actually make this" — not "this is a stock photo".

SETTING: a real lived-in home kitchen counter or table — plain wood, laminate, or a simple cloth. In the soft background: honest everyday context (the baking pan with the rest of the dish, a coffee mug, a crumpled kitchen towel). No luxury marble, no styled props, no perfect staging.

FRAMING: vertical 2:3. The dish fills roughly 70% of the frame at a natural eye-level or slight high angle — the way a person actually photographs their food.

STRICT NEGATIVE: no washed-out colors, no pale flat image, no hazy overexposed highlights, no oversaturation, no HDR, no glossy studio lighting, no perfect symmetry, no flawless surfaces, no editorial styling, no marble, no steam, no smoke, no text, no watermark, no CGI look, no plastic textures, no over-smoothing, no advertising aesthetic.
IMG_PROMPT_2:
A candid ACTION photograph of {title}, shot by a home food blogger in her real kitchen — the moment of serving or cutting, captured naturally, like a photo from a personal recipe blog, never a commercial ad or AI render.

THE MOMENT (pick what fits this dish): a hand lifting a slice or spoonful with the interior visible, cutting the first piece with an ordinary kitchen knife, drizzling sauce from a spoon, or pulling a portion apart. If a hand is visible: it enters the frame naturally from the side with the wrist and part of the forearm visible — NEVER floating fingertips cropped at the frame edge. Realistic adult hand, natural skin texture, correct anatomy, five fingers, maybe a simple ring or slightly flour-dusted — a real person's hand.

The lifted or cut portion shows the honest interior: real crumb with irregular holes, filling that drips a little messily, layers that are not perfectly even. A few crumbs fall — leave them. Thick or creamy foods (jam, sauce, batter, frosting) fall in ONE heavy, slow drop with realistic viscosity — never long stretchy strings.

CAMERA & LOOK: DSLR 50mm f/2.2, focus locked on the lifted/cut portion, the rest falls off naturally. Slight motion feel in the action. Fine natural grain. True-to-life but RICH colors — deep, appetizing, with real contrast; never washed-out, pale, or hazy. Natural white balance (avoid a heavy orange/amber cast), no HDR, no glow, no gloss.

LIGHT: plain daylight from a nearby window, soft and directional. Real soft shadows. Slightly uneven exposure like a genuine indoor photo.

SETTING — clearly DIFFERENT from the hero shot: a simple wooden table or an everyday countertop with honest mess: the knife resting with crumbs on it, the pan nearby, a used fork, a wrinkled napkin. Real home, not a set. No marble, no styled props.

FRAMING: vertical 2:3, closer crop on the action, camera at a natural 45-degree angle like someone photographing while serving.

STRICT NEGATIVE: no washed-out colors, no pale flat image, no hazy overexposed highlights, no oversaturation, no HDR, no studio lighting, no perfect food, no editorial styling, no marble, no steam, no smoke, no text, no watermark, no CGI look, no plastic textures, no over-smoothing, no deformed hands, no extra fingers, no advertising aesthetic.
IMG_PROMPT_3:
A cozy, candid TABLE photograph of {title} just served at a real family table, shot by a home food blogger — it must feel like a genuine everyday moment in a real home, never a magazine spread or AI render.

THE SCENE: the finished dish sits on the table ready to eat, in its baking dish or on a simple serving plate. Around it, honest everyday life: mismatched plates, a glass of water or iced tea, ordinary cutlery, a bowl of the key ingredient, maybe a corner of a chair or a folded newspaper at the frame edge. One portion is already served on a smaller plate — someone couldn't wait. Nothing is styled; things sit where a real family would put them.

CAMERA & LOOK: DSLR 35mm f/2.8, wider framing than a close-up, shot from a seated person's eye level or a relaxed 30-degree angle. Focus on the main dish, surroundings soften naturally. Fine grain, true-to-life but RICH colors — the food's natural colors stay deep and appetizing, the scene has real contrast and depth, never washed-out, pale, or hazy. Natural white balance. No HDR, no glow, no editorial color grading, no flat overexposed brightness.

LIGHT: real daylight from a kitchen or dining room window with soft DIRECTIONAL contrast — warm, cozy, slightly moody food-blog mood. Natural shadows give the table depth; corners of the room fall darker — like a genuine indoor family photo, not a bright white catalog shot. The dish is the brightest, most colorful point of the frame so the eye lands on it first.

SETTING — clearly DIFFERENT from the other two shots: a family dining table (plain wood or with a simple tablecloth), a lived-in home in the background, softly blurred: a chair, a counter, a window. No marble, no props arranged in triangles, no staging.

IMPERFECTIONS ARE REQUIRED: a crumb trail where the dish was cut, a drip on the table, the serving spoon left in the dish, a napkin tossed casually. Perfect = fake.

FRAMING: vertical 2:3, the dish takes about 55-65% of the frame with breathing room around it.

STRICT NEGATIVE: no washed-out colors, no pale flat image, no hazy overexposed highlights, no oversaturation, no HDR, no studio lighting, no perfect symmetry, no editorial styling, no marble, no steam, no smoke, no text, no watermark, no CGI look, no plastic textures, no over-smoothing, no people's faces, no advertising aesthetic.
